title: Self-Healing Agentic Orchestrators for Reliable Tool-Augmented Large Language Model Systems
author: Babu, Rahul Suresh, Agrawal, Adarsh
summary: This paper addresses reliability challenges in tool-augmented LLM systems by proposing a self-healing agentic orchestrator that treats failure recovery as a bounded runtime control problem. Rather than relying solely on model-level reasoning, the orchestrator detects failure signals (tool timeouts, malformed arguments, stale context), maps them to failure classes, and executes targeted recovery actions within explicit resource budgets. Evaluation on a 100-task fault-injection benchmark demonstrates 98.8% task success versus 94.5% for retry-only and 93.8% for full replanning baselines. The work provides empirical evidence that explicit orchestration-level failure handling significantly improves reliability and diagnosability in practical tool-augmented LLM deployments.
content: Computer Science > Artificial Intelligence arXiv:2606.01416 (cs) [Submitted on 31 May 2026] Title: Self-Healing Agentic Orchestrators for Reliable Tool-Augmented Large Language Model Systems Authors: Rahul Suresh Babu , Adarsh Agrawal View a PDF of the paper titled Self-Healing Agentic Orchestrators for Reliable Tool-Augmented Large Language Model Systems, by Rahul Suresh Babu and 1 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: Tool-augmented large language model (LLM) agents rely on orchestration layers that coordinate planning, retrieval, tool invocation, validation, memory, and recovery. In these systems, failures arise not only from model errors, but also from orchestration-level issues such as tool timeouts, malformed arguments, stale context, contradictory evidence, retry loops, and unverified intermediate outputs. This paper presents a self-healing agentic orchestrator that treats reliability as a bounded runtime control problem. The orchestrator maps observable failure signals to inferred failure classes, selects targeted recovery actions under explicit budgets, verifies recovered trajectories, and records observability traces. We evaluate the approach on a 100-task controlled fault-injection benchmark against static workflow, retry-only, ReAct-style, and full-replanning baselines. Self-healing achieves 98.8\% task success, compared with 94.5\% for retry-only and 93.8\% for full replanning. A matched recovery-budget sweep shows that self-healing outperforms retry-only and full replanning at every tested budget, with the largest gap under a single recovery attempt: 94.0\% versus 85.3\% and 88.2\%, respectively. Under a controlled semantic silent-failure setting, verifier-guided self-healing reduces silent failures to 0.0\%, while non-verifying baselines return wrong-but-plausible outputs more often. A compact model-in-the-loop validation shows that the same recovery mechanism can operate when a live tool-calling model performs tool selection, argument generation, and answer synthesis over local fault-injected tools. These results provide controlled evidence that failure-aware, budgeted, and verification-guided orchestration improves reliability and diagnosability in tool-augmented LLM systems. takeaways: Self-healing orchestrators map observable failure signals to recovery actions under budgeted constraints, outperforming retry-only and replanning baselines.
Verification-guided recovery eliminates silent failures (wrong-but-plausible outputs) that non-verifying baselines produce.
Single-recovery-attempt performance shows largest gains: 94% success versus 85.3% (retry-only) and 88.2% (full replanning).
Orchestration-level failures (timeouts, malformed args, stale context) are distinct from model errors and require targeted mechanisms.

title: https://community.openai.com/t/ai-mentor-search-agents-sop-fine-tuning-api-orchestration/1367250
summary: This content appears to be a community forum post title discussing AI mentor search agents, standard operating procedures (SOPs), fine-tuning APIs, and orchestration strategies. However, no substantive content was provided beyond the title. Without the article body, it is impossible to assess the specific thesis, main arguments, or technical findings. A reader seeking information about agent architectures, fine-tuning methodologies, or API orchestration patterns would need access to the full article text to determine whether this resource offers actionable insights or useful technical guidance. The fragmentary nature of what was provided limits meaningful evaluation.
